I Étale morphisms

To simplify the exposition, we assume that all the preschemes considered are locally Noetherian, at least after section 2.

pages.title: Notions of differential calculus
pages.html:
1 Notions of differential calculus

Let \(X\) be a prescheme over \(Y\), let \(\Delta_{X/Y}\) or \(\Delta\) be the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y X\). It is an immersion morphism, hence a closed immersion morphism of \(X\) into an open set \(V\) of \(X\times_Y X\). Let \(\mathscr{I}_X\) be the ideal of the closed sub-prescheme corresponding to the diagonal in \(V\) (N.B. if one wishes to do things intrinsically, without supposing \(X\) separated over \(Y\) — a hypothesis which would be a hoax — one ought to consider the set-theoretic inverse image of \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X}\) in \(X\), and designate by \(\mathscr{I}_X\) the augmentation ideal in this last one...). The sheaf \(\mathscr{I}_X/\mathscr{I}_X^2\) may be regarded as a quasi-coherent sheaf on \(X\), we denote it by \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/Y}\). It is of finite type if \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is of finite type. It behaves well with respect to a base extension \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). One also introduces the sheaves \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times_Y X}/\mathscr{I}_X^{n+1}=\mathscr{P}^n_{X/Y}\), these are sheaves of rings on \(X\), making of \(X\) a prescheme which may be denoted by \(\Delta_{X/Y}^n\) and called the infinitesimal neighbourhood of \(X/Y\). The formalism of it is of a total triviality, although it is rather long1; it would be prudent to speak of it only at the moment when one says something serviceable about it, with the smooth morphisms.

pages.title: Quasi-finite morphisms
pages.html:
2 Quasi-finite morphisms

Proposition 1

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism (N.B. the rings are now Noetherian), \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal of \(A\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is of finite dimension over \(k=A/\mathfrak{m}\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathfrak{m}B\) is an ideal of definition, and \(B/\mathfrak{r}(B)=\mathit{k}(B)\) is an extension of \(k=\mathit{k}(A)\)

  3. (iii)

    The completion \(\widehat{B}\) is finite over the completion \(\widehat{A}\) of \(A\).

One then says that \(B\) is quasi-finite over \(A\). A morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is said to be quasi-finite at \(x\) (or the \(Y\)-prescheme \(f\) is said to be quasi-finite at \(x\)) if \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is quasi-finite over \(\mathscr{O}_{f(x)}\). This also amounts to saying that \(x\) is isolated in its fibre \(f^{-1}(x)\). A morphism is said to be quasi-finite if it is so at every point1.

Corollary 2

If \(A\) is complete, quasi-finite is equivalent to finite.

One could give the usual sorites (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), for quasi-finite morphisms, but this does not seem indispensable here.

pages.title: Unramified or net morphisms
pages.html:
3 Unramified or net morphisms

Proposition 1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type, \(x\in X\), \(y=f(x)\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathscr{O}_x/\mathfrak{m}_y\mathscr{O}_x\) is a finite separable extension of \(\mathit{k}(y)\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/Y}\) is null at \(x\).

  3. (iii)

    The diagonal morphism \(\Delta_{X/Y}\) is an open immersion in a neighbourhood of \(x\).

For the implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), one is at once reduced by Nakayama to the case where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(k')\), where it is well known and moreover trivial from the definition of separable; (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) by a pleasant and easy characterisation of the open immersions, using Krull; (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) since one is again reduced to the case where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) and where the diagonal morphism is everywhere an open immersion. One must then prove that \(X\) is finite with separable ring over \(k\); for this one is reduced to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. But then every closed point of \(X\) is isolated (since identical to the inverse image of the diagonal by the morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_k X\) defined by \(x\)), whence the fact that \(X\) is finite. One may then suppose \(X\) reduced to a point, with ring \(A\), so that \(A\otimes_k A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) is an isomorphism, whence \(A=k\) q.e.d.

Definition 2

  1. a)

    One then says that \(f\) is net, or also unramified, at \(x\), or that \(X\) is net, or also unramified, at \(x\) over \(Y\).

  2. b)

    Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism; one says that it is net, or unramified, or that \(B\) is a net, or unramified, local algebra over \(A\), if \(B/\mathfrak{r}(A)B\) is a finite separable extension of \(A/\mathfrak{r}(A)\), i.e. if \(\mathfrak{r}(A)B=\mathfrak{r}(B)\) and \(\mathit{k}(B)\) is a separable extension of \(\mathit{k}(A)\)1.

Remarks

The fact that \(B\) is net over \(A\) can already be recognised on the completions of \(A\) and of \(B\). Net implies quasi-finite.

Corollary 3

The set of points where \(f\) is net is open.

Corollary 4

Let \(X'\), \(X\) be two preschemes of finite type over \(Y\), and \(g\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a \(Y\)-morphism. If \(X\) is net over \(Y\), the graph morphism \(\Gamma_g\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y X\) is an open immersion.

Indeed, it is the inverse image of the diagonal morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y X\) by

\[ g\times_Y\mathrm{id}_{X'}\colon X'\times_Y X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y X. \]

One may also introduce the annihilator ideal \(\mathfrak{d}_{X/Y}\) of \(\mathit{\Omega}^1_{X/Y}\), called the different ideal of \(X/Y\); it defines a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\) which, set-theoretically, is the set of points where \(X/Y\) is ramified, i.e. non-net.

Proposition 5

  1. (i)

    An immersion is net.

  2. (ii)

    The composite of two net morphisms is net.

  3. (iii)

    Base extension of a net morphism is another one.

This is seen indifferently on (ii) or (iii) (the second seems to me more amusing). One may of course also make it precise, by giving pointwise statements; this is more general only in appearance (except in the framework of definition b)), and tedious. As usual one obtains corollaries:

Corollaries 6

  1. (iv)

    Cartesian product of two net morphisms is another one.

  2. (v)

    If \(gf\) is net, \(f\) is net.

  3. (vi)

    If \(f\) is net, \(f_{\text{\textup{réd}}}\) is net.

Proposition 7

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism; one supposes the residue extension \(\mathit{k}(B)/\mathit{k}(A)\) trivial or \(\mathit{k}(A)\) algebraically closed. In order that \(B/A\) be net, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\widehat{B}\) be (as a \(\widehat{A}\)-algebra) a quotient of \(\widehat{A}\).

Remarks
  1. –

    In the case where one does not suppose the residue extension trivial, one can reduce to this case by making a suitable finite flat extension over \(A\) which destroys the said extension.

  2. –

    Give the example where \(A\) is the local ring of an ordinary double point of a curve, \(B\) that of a point of the normalisation: then \(A\subset B\), \(B\) is net over \(A\) with trivial residue extension, and \(\widehat{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{B}\) is surjective but not injective. One will therefore strengthen the notion of netness.

pages.title: Étale morphisms. Étale coverings
pages.html:
4 Étale morphisms. Étale coverings

We shall admit everything that will be necessary for us concerning flat morphisms; these facts will be proved later, if there is occasion1.

Definition 1

  1. a)

    Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type. One says that \(f\) is étale at \(x\) if \(f\) is flat at \(x\) and unramified (net) at \(x\). One says that \(f\) is étale if it is so at all points. One says that \(X\) is étale at \(x\) over \(Y\), or that it is a \(Y\)-prescheme étale at \(x\), etc.

  2. b)

    Let \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism. One says that \(f\) is étale, or that \(B\) is étale over \(A\), if \(B\) is flat and unramified over \(A\).2

Proposition 2

For \(B/A\) to be étale, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\widehat{B}/\widehat{A}\) be so.

Indeed, it is true separately for “unramified (net)” and for “flat”.

Corollary 3

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be of finite type, and \(x\in X\). The fact that \(f\) is étale at \(x\) depends only on the local homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_{f(x)}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_x\), and even only on the corresponding homomorphism for the completions.

Corollary 4

Suppose that the residual extension \(\mathit{k}(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{k}(B)\) is trivial, or that \(\mathit{k}(A)\) is algebraically closed. Then \(B/A\) is étale \(\widehat{A}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widehat{B}\) is an isomorphism.

One combines flatness and 7.

Proposition 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type. Then the set of points where it is étale is open.

Indeed, it is true separately for “unramified (net)” and “flat”.

This proposition shows that one may drop the “pointwise” statements in the study of morphisms of finite type that are étale somewhere.

Proposition 6

  1. (i)

    An open immersion is étale.

  2. (ii)

    The composite of two étale morphisms is étale.

  3. (iii)

    Base extension.

Indeed, (i) is trivial, and for (ii) and (iii) it suffices to note that it is true for “unramified (net)” and for “flat”. To tell the truth, there are also corresponding statements for local homomorphisms (without any finiteness condition), which in any case will have to appear in the multiplodocus (beginning with the case: unramified (net)).

Corollary 7

A cartesian product of two étale morphisms is likewise.

Corollary 8

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be of finite type over \(Y\), \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) a \(Y\)-morphism. If \(X'\) is unramified over \(Y\) and \(X\) is étale over \(Y\), then \(g\) is étale.

Indeed, \(g\) is the composite of the graph morphism \(\Gamma_g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y X'\), which is an open immersion by 4, and of the projection morphism, which is étale since it is deduced from the étale morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) by the “base change” \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\).

Definition 9

One calls an étale (resp. unramified) covering of \(Y\) a \(Y\)-scheme \(X\) which is finite over \(Y\) and étale (resp. unramified) over \(Y\).

.5cmThe first condition means that \(X\) is defined by a coherent sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(Y\). The second then means that \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free over \(Y\) (resp. nothing at all), and that moreover, for every \(y\in Y\), the fibre \(\mathscr{B}(y)=\mathscr{B}_y\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_y}\mathit{k}(y)\) is a separable algebra (= finite composite of finite separable extensions) over \(\mathit{k}(y)\).

Proposition 10

Let \(X\) be a flat covering of \(Y\) of degree \(n\) (the definition of this term deserved to appear in 9) defined by a coherent locally free sheaf \(\mathscr{B}\) of algebras. One defines in the well-known manner the trace homomorphism \(\mathscr{B}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{A}\) (which is a homomorphism of \(\mathscr{A}\)-modules, where \(\mathscr{A}=\mathscr{O}_Y\)). For \(X\) to be étale, it is necessary that the bilinear form \(\operatorname{tr}_{\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A}}xy\) corresponding to it define an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{B}\) onto \(\mathscr{B}\), or what amounts to the same, that the discriminant section

\[ d_{X/Y} = d_{\mathscr{B}/\mathscr{A}} \in \Gamma(Y,\mathop{\textstyle\bigwedge}\limits^n\check{\mathscr{B}}\otimes_{\mathscr{A}} \mathop{\textstyle\bigwedge}\limits^n\check{\mathscr{B}}) \]

be invertible, or finally that the discriminant ideal defined by this section be the unit ideal.

Indeed, one is reduced to the case where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), and then it is a well-known criterion of separability, and trivial by passage to the algebraic closure of \(k\).

Remark

One will have a less trivial statement further on, when one does not suppose a priori that \(X\) is flat over \(Y\) but makes a hypothesis of normality.

One will have a less trivial statement further on, when one does not suppose a priori that \(X\) is flat over \(Y\) but makes a hypothesis of normality.

footnotes.html: Cf. remorse in III 2.

pages.title: The fundamental property of étale morphisms
pages.html:
5 The fundamental property of étale morphisms

Theorem 1

Let \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type. For \(f\) to be an open immersion, it that it be an étale and radicial morphism.

Recall that radicial means: injective, with radicial residual extensions (and one may also recall that this means that the morphism remains injective under every extension of the base). Necessity is trivial; sufficiency remains. We shall give two different proofs, the first shorter, the second more elementary.

1) A flat morphism is open, hence we may assume (replacing \(Y\) by \(f(X)\)) that \(f\) is a homeomorphism onto. Under every base extension it will remain true that \(f\) is flat, radicial, surjective, hence a homeomorphism, a fortiori closed Therefore \(f\) is proper. Therefore \(f\) is finite (reference: Chevalley's theorem) defined by a coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{B}\) of algebras. \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free, moreover by virtue of the hypothesis it is everywhere of rank \(1\), hence \(X=Y\), q.e.d.

2) We may assume \(Y\) and \(X\) affine. Moreover, we reduce easily to proving this: if \(Y = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(A\) local, and if \(f^{-1}(y)\) is non-empty (\(y\) being the closed point of \(Y\)) then \(X=Y\) (indeed, this will imply that every \(y \in f(X)\) has an open neighbourhood \(U\) such that \(X|U = U\)). We shall have \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\), we want to prove \(A=B\). But for this, we are reduced to proving the analogous assertion by replacing \(A\) by \(\widehat{A}\), \(B\) by \(B \otimes_A \widehat{A}\) (taking into account that \(\widehat{A}\) is faithfully flat over \(A\)). We may therefore assume \(A\) complete. Let \(x\) be the point above \(y\); by corollary 2 \(\mathscr{O}_x\) is finite over \(A\), hence (being flat and radicial over \(A\)) is identical to \(A\). Therefore we have \(X = Y \amalg X^\prime\) (disjoint sum). Since \(X\) is radicial over \(Y\), \(X^\prime\) is empty. We are done. .5cm

Corollary 2

Let \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a closed immersion and étale morphism. If \(X\) is connected, \(f\) is an isomorphism of \(X\) onto a connected component of \(Y\).

Indeed, \(f\) is also an open immersion. We deduce from this:

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be an unramified (net) \(Y\)-scheme, \(Y\) connected. Then every section of \(X\) over \(Y\) is an isomorphism of \(Y\) onto a connected component of \(X\). There is therefore a bijective correspondence between the set of these sections, and the set of connected components \(X_i\) of \(X\) such that the projection \(X_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is an isomorphism, (or, what amounts to the same by 1, surjective and radicial). In particular, a section is known once one knows its value at a point.

Only the first assertion requires a proof; by 2 it suffices to remark that a section is a closed immersion (since \(X\) is separated over \(Y\)) and étale by virtue of 8.

Corollary 4

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two preschemes over \(S\), \(X\) unramified (net) and separated over \(S\) and \(Y\) connected. Let \(f\), \(g\) be two \(S\)-morphisms from \(Y\) into \(X\), \(y\) a point of \(Y\); suppose \(f(y) = g(y) = x\) and the residual homomorphisms \(\mathit{k}(x) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathit{k}(y)\) defined by \(f\) and \(g\) identical (“\(f\) and \(g\) coincide geometrically at \(y\)”). Then \(f\) and \(g\) are identical.

This follows from 3 by reducing to the case where \(Y=S\), by replacing \(X\) by \(X \times_S Y\).

Here is a particularly important variant of 3.

Theorem 5

Let \(S\) be a prescheme, \(X\) and \(Y\) two \(S\)-preschemes, \(S_0\) a closed sub-prescheme of \(S\) having the same underlying space as \(S\), \(X_0 = X \times_S S_0\) and \(Y_0 = Y \times_S S_0\) the “restrictions” of \(X\) and \(Y\) to \(S_0\). Suppose \(X\) étale over \(S\). Then the natural map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(Y,X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{S_0}(Y_0,X_0) \]

is bijective.

Once again we are reduced to the case where \(Y=S\), and then this follows from the “topological” description of the sections of \(X/Y\) given in 3.

Scholium

This result comprises an assertion of uniqueness and of existence of morphisms. It can also be expressed (when \(X\) and \(Y\) are both taken étale over \(S\)) by saying that the functor \(X \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X_0\) from the category of étale \(S\)-schemes to the category of étale \(S_0\)-schemes is fully faithful, i.e. establishes an equivalence of the first with a full subcategory of the second. We shall see below that it is even an equivalence of the first and the second (which will be a theorem of existence of étale \(S\)-schemes).

The following form, apparently more general, of 5. is often convenient:

Corollary 6 (“Extension theorem for liftings”)

Consider a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]&{Y_0}\ar[l]\ar[d]\\{S}&{Y}\ar[l]} \]

of morphisms, where \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is étale and \(Y_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a bijective closed immersion. Then one can find a unique morphism \(Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) which renders the two corresponding triangles commutative.

Indeed, replacing \(S\) by \(Y\) and \(X\) by \(X \times_S Y\), we are reduced to the case where \(Y=S\), and then it is the particular case of 5 for \(Y=S\).

Let us also point out the following immediate consequence of 1 (which we did not give as corollary 1 in order not to interrupt the line of ideas developed following 1):

Proposition 7

Let \(X\), \(X^\prime\) be two preschemes of finite type and flat over \(Y\), and let \(g\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\prime\) be a \(Y\)-morphism. For \(g\) to be an open immersion (resp. an isomorphism) it is necessary and suffices that for every \(y\in Y\), the morphism induced on the fibres

\[ g \otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y)\colon X \otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\prime \otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y) \]

be so.

It suffices to prove sufficiency; since this is true for the notion of surjection, we are reduced to the case of an open immersion. By 1, it is necessary to verify that \(g\) is radicial, which is trivial, and that it is étale, which follows from corollary 9 below.

Corollary 8

(should be moved to 3) Let \(X\) and \(X^\prime\) be two \(Y\)-preschemes, \(g\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\prime\) a \(Y\)-morphism, \(x\) a point of \(X\) and \(y\) its projection onto \(Y\). For \(g\) to be quasi-finite (resp. unramified (net)) at \(x\), it that the same hold for \(g\otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y)\).

Indeed, the two algebras over \(k\big(g(x)\big)\) that one must look at to be sure that one indeed has a quasi-finite resp. unramified (net) morphism at \(x\) are the same for \(g\) and \(g \otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y)\).

Corollary 9

With the notation of 8, suppose \(X\) and \(X^\prime\) flat and of finite type over \(Y\). For \(g\) to be flat (resp. étale) at \(x\), it that \(g \otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y)\) be so.

For “flat” the statement is included only for the record, it is one of the fundamental criteria of flatness1. For étale, this follows from it; taking into account of 8.

pages.title: Application to étale extensions of complete local rings
pages.html:
6 Application to étale extensions of complete local rings

This article is a particular case of results on formal preschemes, which ought to appear in the multiplodoque. Nevertheless, we get off more cheaply here, i.e. without the explicit local determination of étale morphisms in 7 (using the Main Theorem). This is perhaps a sufficient reason to keep the present article (even in the multiplodoque) in this place.

Theorem 1

Let \(A\) be a complete local ring (Noetherian of course), with residue field \(k\). For every \(A\)-algebra \(B\), let \(R(B) = B \otimes_A k\) considered as a \(k\)-algebra; it thus depends functorially on \(B\). Then \(R\) defines an equivalence of the category of \(A\)-algebras finite and étale over \(A\) with the category of algebras of finite rank and separable over \(k\).

First of all, the functor in question is fully faithful, as follows from the more general fact:

Corollary 2

Let \(B\), \(B^\prime\) be two \(A\)-algebras finite over \(A\). If \(B\) is étale over \(A\), then the canonical map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\textup{$A$-alg}}(B,B^\prime) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\textup{$k$-alg}}\big(R(B),R(B^\prime)\big) \]

is bijective.

We are reduced to the case where \(A\) is Artinian (by replacing \(A\) by \(A/\mathfrak{m}^n\)), and then it is a particular case of 5.

It remains to prove that for every finite and separable \(k\)-algebra (why not say: étale, it is shorter) \(L\), there exists a \(B\) étale over \(A\) such that \(R(B)\) is isomorphic to \(L\). We may suppose that \(L\) is a separable extension of \(k\); as such it admits a generator \(x\), i.e. is isomorphic to an algebra \(k[t]/Fk[t]\) where \(F \in k[t]\) is a monic polynomial. We lift \(F\) to a monic polynomial \(F_1\) in \(A[t]\), and we take \(B = A[t]/F_1A[t]\).

pages.title: Local construction of unramified and étale morphisms
pages.html:
7 Local construction of unramified and étale morphisms

Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(B\) a finite algebra over \(A\), \(u\) a generator of \(B\) over \(A\), \(F \in A[t]\) such that \(F(u) = 0\) (we do not assume \(F\) monic), \(u^\prime = F^\prime(u)\) (where \(F^\prime\) is the derivative polynomial), \(\mathfrak{q}\) a prime ideal of \(B\) not containing \(u^\prime\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) its trace on \(A\). Then \(B_\mathfrak{q}\) is unramified (net) over \(A_\mathfrak{p}\).

In other words, setting \(Y = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X= \operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(X_{u^\prime} = \operatorname{Spec}(B_{u^\prime})\), \(X_{u^\prime}\) is un ramified over \(Y\). The statement follows from the following, more precise one:

Corollary 2

The different ideal of \(B/A\) contains \(u^\prime B\), and is equal to it if the natural homomorphism \(A[t]/FA[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) (sending \(t\) to \(u\)) is an isomorphism.

Let \(J\) be the kernel of the homomorphism \(C = A[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\); this kernel contains \(FA[t]\), and is equal to it in the second case considered in 2. Since it is surjective, \(\Omega^1_{B/A}\) is identified with the quotient of \(\Omega^1_{C/A}\) by the submodule generated by \(J\Omega^1_{C/A}\) and \(d(J)\) (we ought to have made explicit in 1 the definition of the homomorphism \(d\), and the computation of \(\Omega^1\) for a polynomial algebra). Identifying \(\Omega^1_{C/A}\) with \(C\) by means of the basis \(dt\), one finds \(B/B\cdot J^\prime\); hence the different is generated by the set \(J^\prime\) of the images in \(B\) of the derivatives of the \(G \in J\), (and it suffices to take \(G\) generating \(J\)). Since \(F\in J\), resp. \(F\) is a generator of \(J\), we are done. (N.B. One should put 2 as prop. and 1 as corollary). One finds:

Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 1, assuming \(F\) monic and that \(A[t]/FA[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, in order that \(B_\mathfrak{q}\) be étale over \(A_\mathfrak{p}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{q}\) not contain \(u^\prime\).

Indeed, since \(B\) is flat over \(A\), étale is equivalent to unramified, and one may apply 2.

Corollary 4

Under the conditions of 3, in order that \(B\) be étale over \(A\) it is necessary and sufficient that \(u^\prime\) be invertible, or again that the ideal generated by \(F\), \(F^\prime\) in \(A[t]\) be the unit ideal.

The last criterion follows from the first and from Nakayama (in \(B\)).

A monic polynomial \(F\in A[t]\) having the property stated in corollary 4 is called a separable polynomial (if \(F\) is not monic, one would have to require at least that the coefficient of its leading term be invertible; in the case where \(A\) is a field, one recovers the usual definition).

Corollary 5

Let \(B\) be a finite algebra over the local ring \(A\). Suppose that \(K(A)\) is infinite or that \(B\) is local. Let \(n\) be the rank of \(L = B \otimes_A K(A)\) over \(K(A) = k\). In order that \(B\) be unramified (resp. étale) over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be isomorphic to a quotient of (resp. isomorphic to) \(A[t]/FA[t]\), where \(F\) is a monic separable polynomial, which one may suppose (resp. which is necessarily) of degree \(n\).

It only remains to prove necessity. Suppose \(B\) unramified over \(A\), hence \(L\) separable over \(k\); it then follows from the hypothesis made that \(L/k\) admits a generator \(\xi\), hence the \(\xi^i\) (\(0 \leqslant i < n\)) form a basis of \(L\) over \(k\). Let \(u\in B\) lift \(\xi\); then by Nakayama the \(u^i\) (\(0\leqslant i < n\)) generate the \(A\)-module \(B\) (resp. form a basis of it), in particular one may find a monic polynomial \(F \in A[t]\) such that \(F(u)= 0\), and \(B\) will be isomorphic to a quotient of (resp. isomorphic to) \(A[t]/FA[t]\). Finally, by virtue of 4. applied to \(L/k\), \(F\) and \(F^\prime\) generate \(A[t]\) modulo \(\mathfrak{m} A[t]\), hence (by Nakayama in \(A[t]/FA[t]\)) \(F\) and \(F^\prime\) generate \(A[t]\); we are done.

Theorem 6

Let \(A\) be a local ring, \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\) a local homomorphism such that \(\mathscr{O}\) is isomorphic to a localized algebra of an algebra of finite type over \(A\). Suppose \(\mathscr{O}\) unramified over \(A\). Then one can find an \(A\)-algebra \(B\), integral over \(A\), a maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\) of \(B\), a generator \(u\) of \(B\) over \(A\), a monic polynomial \(F\in A[t]\), such that \(\mathfrak{n} \not\ni F^\prime(u)\) and such that \(\mathscr{O}\) is isomorphic (as an \(A\)-algebra) to \(B_{\mathfrak{n}}\). If \(\mathscr{O}\) is étale over \(A\), one may take \(B=A[t]/FA[t]\).

(Of course, one has here conditions that are also sufficient...)

Let us first point out the pleasant corollaries:

Corollary 7

In order that \(\mathscr{O}\) be unramified over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{O}\) be isomorphic to the quotient of an analogous algebra that is étale over \(A\).

Indeed, one will take \(\mathscr{O}^\prime = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), where \(B^\prime = A[t]/FA[t]\) and where \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\) is the inverse image of \(\mathfrak{n}\) in \(B^\prime\).

Corollary 8

Let \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type, \(x\in X\). In order that \(f\) be unramified at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(f|U\) factors as \(U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), where the first arrow is a closed immersion and the second an étale morphism.

This is a mere translation of 7.

Let us show how the jargon of 6 follows from the main statement: indeed, by 7 there exists an epimorphism \(\mathscr{O}^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\), where \(\mathscr{O}\) has the desired properties; but since \(\mathscr{O}^\prime\) and \(\mathscr{O}\) are étale over \(A\), the morphism \(\mathscr{O}^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\) is étale by 8, hence an isomorphism.

Proof of 6

It takes up again a proof from the Chevalley seminar. By the Main Theorem we shall have \(\mathscr{O} = B_{\mathfrak{n}}\), where \(B\) is a finite algebra over \(A\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) is a maximal ideal of it. Then \(B/\mathfrak{n} = K(\mathscr{O})\) is a separable, hence monogenic, extension of \(k\); if \(\mathfrak{n}_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant r\)) are the maximal ideals of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\), there exists then an element \(u\) of \(B\) which belongs to all the \(\mathfrak{n}_i\), and whose image in \(B/\mathfrak{n}\) is a generator of it. Now \(B/\mathfrak{n} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n} B_\mathfrak{n} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) (where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of \(A\)). Let us admit for a moment the

Lemma 9

Let \(A\) be a local ring, \(B\) a finite algebra over \(A\), \(\mathfrak{n}\) a maximal ideal of \(B\), \(u\) an element of \(B\) whose image in \(B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) generates it as an algebra over \(k = A/\mathfrak{m}\), and which lies in all the maximal ideals of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\). Let \(B^\prime = B[u]\), \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime = \mathfrak{n} B^\prime\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_\mathfrak{n}\) is an isomorphism.

Lemma 10

(should have appeared as a corollary to 1 before 5, which it implies). Let \(B\) be a finite algebra over \(A\) generated by an element \(u\), let \(\mathfrak{n}\) be a maximal ideal of \(B\) such that \(B_\mathfrak{n}\) is unramified over \(A\). Then there exists a monic polynomial \(F \in A[t]\) such that \(F(u) = 0\) and \(F^\prime(u) \notin \mathfrak{n}\).

Indeed, let \(n\) be the rank of the \(k\)-algebra \(L = B \otimes_A k\), by Nakayama there exists a monic polynomial of degree \(n\) in \(A[t]\), such that \(F(u) = 0\). Let \(f\) be the polynomial deduced from \(F\) by reduction mod \(\mathfrak{m}\), then \(L\) is \(k\)-isomorphic to \(k[t]/fk[t]\), so by 3 \(f^\prime(\xi)\) is not contained in the maximal ideal of \(L\) which corresponds to \(\mathfrak{n}\) (\(\xi\) denoting the image of \(t\) in \(L\), i.e. the image of \(u\) in \(L\)). Since \(f^\prime(\xi)\) is the image of \(F^\prime(u)\), we are done.

Theorem 6 now results from the conjunction of 9 and 10. It remains to prove 9. Set \(S^\prime = B^\prime - \mathfrak{n}^\prime\), so that \(B^\prime {S^\prime}^{-1} = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\).

\[ \begin{array}{ccccc} B&\longrightarrow&B{S^\prime}^{-1}&\longrightarrow&BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\\ \Big\uparrow&&\Big\uparrow&&\\ B^\prime&\longrightarrow&B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1} \rlap{\hbox{$\displaystyle \ = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}$}}&& \end{array} \]

Likewise let \(S=B-\mathfrak{n}\), so that \(BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\), we thus have a natural homomorphism \(B{S^\prime}^{-1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\), let us prove that it is an isomorphism, i.e. that the elements of \(S\) are invertible in \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\), i.e. that every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of this latter does not meet \(S\), i.e. induces \(\mathfrak{n}\) on \(B\). Indeed, since \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\) is finite over \(B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1} = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) induces the unique maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime B_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\) of \(B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), hence induces the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\) of \(B^\prime\); since \(B\) is finite over \(B^\prime\), the ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) induced by \(\mathfrak{p}\), being above \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\), is necessarily maximal, and does not contain \(u\), hence is identical to \(\mathfrak{n}\). (We have just used that \(u\) belongs to every maximal ideal of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\)). Let us now prove that \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\) equals \(B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1}\): since it is finite over this latter, we are reduced by Nakayama to proving the equality mod \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime B {S^\prime}^{-1}\) and a fortiori it suffices to prove the equality mod \(\mathfrak{m} B{S^\prime}^{-1}\); now \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}/\mathfrak{m} B{S^\prime}^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) is generated over \(k\) by \(u\) (here one uses the other property of \(u\)), hence the image of \(B^\prime\) (and a fortiori of \(B^\prime {S^\prime}^{-1}\)) therein is everything (as a subring containing \(k\) and the image of \(u\)).

Remark

One should be able to state theorem 6 for a ring \(\mathscr{O}\) which is only semi-local, so as to also cover 5: one will make the hypothesis that \(\mathscr{O}/\mathfrak{m}\mathscr{O}\) is a monogenic \(k\)-algebra; one will thus be able to find a \(u\in B\) whose image in \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is a generator, and belonging to all the maximal ideals of \(B\) not coming from \(\mathscr{O}\). Lemmas 9 and 10 should adapt without difficulty. More generally, ...

blocks.html: 7 Local construction of unramified and étale morphisms

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian ring, \(B\) a finite algebra over \(A\), \(u\) a generator of \(B\) over \(A\), \(F \in A[t]\) such that \(F(u) = 0\) (we do not assume \(F\) monic), \(u^\prime = F^\prime(u)\) (where \(F^\prime\) is the derivative polynomial), \(\mathfrak{q}\) a prime ideal of \(B\) not containing \(u^\prime\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) its trace on \(A\). Then \(B_\mathfrak{q}\) is unramified (net) over \(A_\mathfrak{p}\).

In other words, setting \(Y = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X= \operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(X_{u^\prime} = \operatorname{Spec}(B_{u^\prime})\), \(X_{u^\prime}\) is un ramified over \(Y\). The statement follows from the following, more precise one:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

The different ideal of \(B/A\) contains \(u^\prime B\), and is equal to it if the natural homomorphism \(A[t]/FA[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) (sending \(t\) to \(u\)) is an isomorphism.

Let \(J\) be the kernel of the homomorphism \(C = A[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\); this kernel contains \(FA[t]\), and is equal to it in the second case considered in 2. Since it is surjective, \(\Omega^1_{B/A}\) is identified with the quotient of \(\Omega^1_{C/A}\) by the submodule generated by \(J\Omega^1_{C/A}\) and \(d(J)\) (we ought to have made explicit in 1 the definition of the homomorphism \(d\), and the computation of \(\Omega^1\) for a polynomial algebra). Identifying \(\Omega^1_{C/A}\) with \(C\) by means of the basis \(dt\), one finds \(B/B\cdot J^\prime\); hence the different is generated by the set \(J^\prime\) of the images in \(B\) of the derivatives of the \(G \in J\), (and it suffices to take \(G\) generating \(J\)). Since \(F\in J\), resp. \(F\) is a generator of \(J\), we are done. (N.B. One should put 2 as prop. and 1 as corollary). One finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 1, assuming \(F\) monic and that \(A[t]/FA[t] \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is an isomorphism, in order that \(B_\mathfrak{q}\) be étale over \(A_\mathfrak{p}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{q}\) not contain \(u^\prime\).
Indeed, since \(B\) is flat over \(A\), étale is equivalent to unramified, and one may apply 2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Under the conditions of 3, in order that \(B\) be étale over \(A\) it is necessary and sufficient that \(u^\prime\) be invertible, or again that the ideal generated by \(F\), \(F^\prime\) in \(A[t]\) be the unit ideal.
The last criterion follows from the first and from Nakayama (in \(B\)).

A monic polynomial \(F\in A[t]\) having the property stated in corollary 4 is called a separable polynomial (if \(F\) is not monic, one would have to require at least that the coefficient of its leading term be invertible; in the case where \(A\) is a field, one recovers the usual definition).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(B\) be a finite algebra over the local ring \(A\). Suppose that \(K(A)\) is infinite or that \(B\) is local. Let \(n\) be the rank of \(L = B \otimes_A K(A)\) over \(K(A) = k\). In order that \(B\) be unramified (resp. étale) over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be isomorphic to a quotient of (resp. isomorphic to) \(A[t]/FA[t]\), where \(F\) is a monic separable polynomial, which one may suppose (resp. which is necessarily) of degree \(n\).

It only remains to prove necessity. Suppose \(B\) unramified over \(A\), hence \(L\) separable over \(k\); it then follows from the hypothesis made that \(L/k\) admits a generator \(\xi\), hence the \(\xi^i\) (\(0 \leqslant i < n\)) form a basis of \(L\) over \(k\). Let \(u\in B\) lift \(\xi\); then by Nakayama the \(u^i\) (\(0\leqslant i < n\)) generate the \(A\)-module \(B\) (resp. form a basis of it), in particular one may find a monic polynomial \(F \in A[t]\) such that \(F(u)= 0\), and \(B\) will be isomorphic to a quotient of (resp. isomorphic to) \(A[t]/FA[t]\). Finally, by virtue of 4. applied to \(L/k\), \(F\) and \(F^\prime\) generate \(A[t]\) modulo \(\mathfrak{m} A[t]\), hence (by Nakayama in \(A[t]/FA[t]\)) \(F\) and \(F^\prime\) generate \(A[t]\); we are done.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6

Let \(A\) be a local ring, \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\) a local homomorphism such that \(\mathscr{O}\) is isomorphic to a localized algebra of an algebra of finite type over \(A\). Suppose \(\mathscr{O}\) unramified over \(A\). Then one can find an \(A\)-algebra \(B\), integral over \(A\), a maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\) of \(B\), a generator \(u\) of \(B\) over \(A\), a monic polynomial \(F\in A[t]\), such that \(\mathfrak{n} \not\ni F^\prime(u)\) and such that \(\mathscr{O}\) is isomorphic (as an \(A\)-algebra) to \(B_{\mathfrak{n}}\). If \(\mathscr{O}\) is étale over \(A\), one may take \(B=A[t]/FA[t]\).
(Of course, one has here conditions that are also sufficient...)
Let us first point out the pleasant corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

In order that \(\mathscr{O}\) be unramified over \(A\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{O}\) be isomorphic to the quotient of an analogous algebra that is étale over \(A\).

Indeed, one will take \(\mathscr{O}^\prime = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), where \(B^\prime = A[t]/FA[t]\) and where \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\) is the inverse image of \(\mathfrak{n}\) in \(B^\prime\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of finite type, \(x\in X\). In order that \(f\) be unramified at \(x\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(f|U\) factors as \(U \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), where the first arrow is a closed immersion and the second an étale morphism.
This is a mere translation of 7.

Let us show how the jargon of 6 follows from the main statement: indeed, by 7 there exists an epimorphism \(\mathscr{O}^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\), where \(\mathscr{O}\) has the desired properties; but since \(\mathscr{O}^\prime\) and \(\mathscr{O}\) are étale over \(A\), the morphism \(\mathscr{O}^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}\) is étale by 8, hence an isomorphism.
Proof of 6

It takes up again a proof from the Chevalley seminar. By the Main Theorem we shall have \(\mathscr{O} = B_{\mathfrak{n}}\), where \(B\) is a finite algebra over \(A\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) is a maximal ideal of it. Then \(B/\mathfrak{n} = K(\mathscr{O})\) is a separable, hence monogenic, extension of \(k\); if \(\mathfrak{n}_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant r\)) are the maximal ideals of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\), there exists then an element \(u\) of \(B\) which belongs to all the \(\mathfrak{n}_i\), and whose image in \(B/\mathfrak{n}\) is a generator of it. Now \(B/\mathfrak{n} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{n} B_\mathfrak{n} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) (where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of \(A\)). Let us admit for a moment the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9

Let \(A\) be a local ring, \(B\) a finite algebra over \(A\), \(\mathfrak{n}\) a maximal ideal of \(B\), \(u\) an element of \(B\) whose image in \(B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) generates it as an algebra over \(k = A/\mathfrak{m}\), and which lies in all the maximal ideals of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\). Let \(B^\prime = B[u]\), \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime = \mathfrak{n} B^\prime\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_\mathfrak{n}\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 10

(should have appeared as a corollary to 1 before 5, which it implies). Let \(B\) be a finite algebra over \(A\) generated by an element \(u\), let \(\mathfrak{n}\) be a maximal ideal of \(B\) such that \(B_\mathfrak{n}\) is unramified over \(A\). Since \(f^\prime(\xi)\) is the image of \(F^\prime(u)\), we are done.

Theorem 6 now results from the conjunction of 9 and 10. It remains to prove 9. Set \(S^\prime = B^\prime - \mathfrak{n}^\prime\), so that \(B^\prime {S^\prime}^{-1} = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\).

\[ \begin{array}{ccccc} B&\longrightarrow&B{S^\prime}^{-1}&\longrightarrow&BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\\ \Big\uparrow&&\Big\uparrow&&\\ B^\prime&\longrightarrow&B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1} \rlap{\hbox{$\displaystyle \ = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}$}}&& \end{array} \]

Likewise let \(S=B-\mathfrak{n}\), so that \(BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\), we thus have a natural homomorphism \(B{S^\prime}^{-1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} BS^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}\), let us prove that it is an isomorphism, i.e. that the elements of \(S\) are invertible in \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\), i.e. that every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of this latter does not meet \(S\), i.e. induces \(\mathfrak{n}\) on \(B\). Indeed, since \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\) is finite over \(B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1} = B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), \(\mathfrak{p}\) induces the unique maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime B_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\) of \(B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}^\prime}\), hence induces the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\) of \(B^\prime\); since \(B\) is finite over \(B^\prime\), the ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) induced by \(\mathfrak{p}\), being above \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime\), is necessarily maximal, and does not contain \(u\), hence is identical to \(\mathfrak{n}\). (We have just used that \(u\) belongs to every maximal ideal of \(B\) distinct from \(\mathfrak{n}\)). Let us now prove that \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}\) equals \(B^\prime{S^\prime}^{-1}\): since it is finite over this latter, we are reduced by Nakayama to proving the equality mod \(\mathfrak{n}^\prime B {S^\prime}^{-1}\) and a fortiori it suffices to prove the equality mod \(\mathfrak{m} B{S^\prime}^{-1}\); now \(B{S^\prime}^{-1}/\mathfrak{m} B{S^\prime}^{-1} = B_\mathfrak{n}/\mathfrak{m} B_\mathfrak{n}\) is generated over \(k\) by \(u\) (here one uses the other property of \(u\)), hence the image of \(B^\prime\) (and a fortiori of \(B^\prime {S^\prime}^{-1}\)) therein is everything (as a subring containing \(k\) and the image of \(u\)).

blocks.html:
Remark

One should be able to state theorem 6 for a ring \(\mathscr{O}\) which is only semi-local, so as to also cover 5: one will make the hypothesis that \(\mathscr{O}/\mathfrak{m}\mathscr{O}\) is a monogenic \(k\)-algebra; one will thus be able to find a \(u\in B\) whose image in \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is a generator, and belonging to all the maximal ideals of \(B\) not coming from \(\mathscr{O}\). Lemmas 9 and 10 should adapt without difficulty. More generally, ...

pages.title:
Infinitesimal lifting of étale schemes.
Application to formal schemes
pages.html:
8 Infinitesimal lifting of étale schemes. Application to formal schemes

Proposition 1

Let \(Y\) be a prescheme, \(Y_0\) a sub-prescheme, \(X_0\) an étale \(Y_0\)-scheme, \(x\) a point of \(X_0\). Then there exists an étale \(Y\)-scheme \(X\), a neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(x\) in \(X_0\), and a \(Y_0\)-isomorphism \(U_0 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X \times_Y Y_0\).

Indeed, let \(y\) be the projection of \(x\) in \(Y_0\); applying 6 to the local étale homomorphism \(A_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_0\) of the local rings of \(y\) and \(x\) in \(Y_0\) and \(X_0\): one finds an isomorphism

\[ B_0 = {C_0}_{\mathfrak{n}_0}\qquad C_0 = A_0[t]/F_0A_0[t] \]

where \(F_0\) is a monic polynomial and \(\mathfrak{n}_0\) is a maximal ideal of \(C_0\) not containing the class of \(F^\prime_0(t)\) in \(C_0\). Let \(A\) be the local ring of \(y\) in \(Y\), let \(F\) be a monic polynomial in \(A[t]\) giving \(F_0\) via the surjective homomorphism \(A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_0\) (one lifts the coefficients of \(F_0\)), let finally \(C = A[t]/FA[t]\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) the maximal ideal of \(C\) inverse image of \(\mathfrak{n}_0\) under the natural epimorphism \(C \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C \otimes_A A_0 = C_0\). Set

\[ B = C_\mathfrak{n}. \]

It is immediate by construction and 1 that \(B\) is étale over \(A\), and that one has an isomorphism \(B \otimes_A A_0 = A_0\). It is known (Chap. I) that there exists a \(Y\)-scheme of finite type \(X\) and a point \(z\) of \(X\) above \(y\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_z\) is \(A\)-isomorphic to \(C\); since the latter is étale over \(A = \mathscr{O}_y\), one may (by taking \(X\) small enough) assume that \(X\) is étale over \(Y\). Let \(X_0^\prime = X \times_Y Y_0\); then the local ring of \(z\) in \(X_0^\prime\) is identified with \(\mathscr{O}_z \otimes_A A_0 = B \otimes_A A_0\), hence is isomorphic to \(B_0\). This isomorphism is defined by an isomorphism of a neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(x\) in \(X_0\) onto a neighbourhood of \(z\) in \(X_0^\prime\) (loc. cit.), which one may assume identical to \(X_0^\prime\) by taking \(X\) small enough. We are done.

Corollary 2

Analogous statement for étale coverings, assuming the residue field \(\mathit{k}(y)\) infinite.

The proof is the same, 5 replacing 6.

Theorem 3

The functor considered in 5 is an equivalence of categories.

By virtue of theorem 5, it remains to show that every étale \(S_0\)-scheme \(X_0\) is isomorphic to an \(S_0\)-scheme \(X \times_S S_0\), where \(X\) is an étale \(S\)-scheme. The topological space underlying \(X\) will necessarily have to be identical to that of \(X_0\), \(X_0\) being moreover identified with a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\). The problem is therefore equivalent to the following: to find, on the topological space \(|X_0|\) underlying \(X_0\), a sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X\) over \(f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S)\) (where \(f_0\) is the projection \(X_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\), regarded here as a continuous map of the underlying spaces), making \(|X_0|\) into an étale \(S\)-prescheme \(X\), and a homomorphism of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}\), compatible with the homomorphism \(f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_{S_0})\) on the sheaves of scalars, inducing an isomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \otimes_{f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S)} f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}\). (Then \(X\) will be an étale \(S\)-prescheme reducing along \(X_0\), hence will be separated over \(S\) since \(X_0\) is so over \(S_0\), and \(X\) answers the question). If moreover \((U_i)\) is a covering of \(X_0\) by opens, and if one has found a solution of the problem in each of the \(U_i\), it follows from the uniqueness theorem 5 that these solutions glue (i.e. the sheaves of algebras which define them, equipped with their augmentation homomorphisms, glue), and one immediately verifies that the ringed space thus constructed over \(S\) is an étale \(S\)-prescheme \(X\) equipped with an isomorphism \(X \times_S S_0 \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X_0\). It therefore suffices to find a solution locally, which is assured by 1.

Corollary 4

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian formal prescheme, equipped with an ideal of definition \(J\), let \(S_0 = \big(|S|,\mathscr{O}_S/\mathscr{J}\big)\) be the corresponding ordinary prescheme. Then the functor \(\mathfrak{X} \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathfrak{X} \times_S S_0\) from the category of étale coverings of \(S\) to the category of étale coverings of \(S_0\) is an equivalence of categories.

Of course, by an étale covering of a formal prescheme \(S\) one calls a covering of \(S\), i.e. a formal prescheme over \(S\) defined by means of a coherent sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\), such that \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free and such that the residual fibres \(\mathscr{B}_s \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_s} \mathit{k}(s)\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) are separable algebras over \(\mathit{k}(s)\). If one denotes by \(S_n\) the ordinary prescheme \(\big(|S|,\mathscr{O}_S/J^{n+1}\big)\), the datum of a coherent sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(S\) is equivalent to the datum of a sequence of coherent sheaves of algebras \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\), equipped with a transitive system of homomorphisms \(\mathscr{B}_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{B}_n\) (\(m \geqslant n\)) defining isomorphisms \(\mathscr{B}_m \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_m}} \mathscr{O}_{S_n} \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{B}_n\). It is immediate that \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free if and only if the \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\) are, and that the condition of separability is satisfied if and only if it is so for \(\mathscr{B}_0\), or again for all the \(\mathscr{B}_n\). Thus, \(\mathscr{B}\) is étale over \(S\) if and only if the \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\) are. Taking this into account, 4 follows immediately from 3.

Remark

It was not necessary in 4 to restrict to the case of coverings. It is however the only one used for the moment.

\[ B = C_\mathfrak{n}. \]

I) that there exists a \(Y\)-scheme of finite type \(X\) and a point \(z\) of \(X\) above \(y\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_z\) is \(A\)-isomorphic to \(C\); since the latter is étale over \(A = \mathscr{O}_y\), one may (by taking \(X\) small enough) assume that \(X\) is étale over \(Y\). By virtue of theorem 5, it remains to show that every étale \(S_0\)-scheme \(X_0\) is isomorphic to an \(S_0\)-scheme \(X \times_S S_0\), where \(X\) is an étale \(S\)-scheme. The topological space underlying \(X\) will necessarily have to be identical to that of \(X_0\), \(X_0\) being moreover identified with a closed sub-prescheme of \(X\). The problem is therefore equivalent to the following: to find, on the topological space \(|X_0|\) underlying \(X_0\), a sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X\) over \(f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S)\) (where \(f_0\) is the projection \(X_0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S_0\), regarded here as a continuous map of the underlying spaces), making \(|X_0|\) into an étale \(S\)-prescheme \(X\), and a homomorphism of algebras \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}\), compatible with the homomorphism \(f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_{S_0})\) on the sheaves of scalars, inducing an isomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \otimes_{f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_S)} f_0^*(\mathscr{O}_{S_0}) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}\). (Then \(X\) will be an étale \(S\)-prescheme reducing along \(X_0\), hence will be separated over \(S\) since \(X_0\) is so over \(S_0\), and \(X\) answers the question). If moreover \((U_i)\) is a covering of \(X_0\) by opens, and if one has found a solution of the problem in each of the \(U_i\), it follows from the uniqueness theorem 5 that these solutions glue (i.e. the sheaves of algebras which define them, equipped with their augmentation homomorphisms, glue), and one immediately verifies that the ringed space thus constructed over \(S\) is an étale \(S\)-prescheme \(X\) equipped with an isomorphism \(X \times_S S_0 \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} X_0\). It therefore suffices to find a solution locally, which is assured by 1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian formal prescheme, equipped with an ideal of definition \(J\), let \(S_0 = \big(|S|,\mathscr{O}_S/\mathscr{J}\big)\) be the corresponding ordinary prescheme. Then the functor \(\mathfrak{X} \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \mathfrak{X} \times_S S_0\) from the category of étale coverings of \(S\) to the category of étale coverings of \(S_0\) is an equivalence of categories.

Of course, by an étale covering of a formal prescheme \(S\) one calls a covering of \(S\), i.e. a formal prescheme over \(S\) defined by means of a coherent sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\), such that \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free and such that the residual fibres \(\mathscr{B}_s \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_s} \mathit{k}(s)\) of \(\mathscr{B}\) are separable algebras over \(\mathit{k}(s)\). If one denotes by \(S_n\) the ordinary prescheme \(\big(|S|,\mathscr{O}_S/J^{n+1}\big)\), the datum of a coherent sheaf of algebras \(\mathscr{B}\) over \(S\) is equivalent to the datum of a sequence of coherent sheaves of algebras \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\), equipped with a transitive system of homomorphisms \(\mathscr{B}_m\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{B}_n\) (\(m \geqslant n\)) defining isomorphisms \(\mathscr{B}_m \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_m}} \mathscr{O}_{S_n} \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathscr{B}_n\). It is immediate that \(\mathscr{B}\) is locally free if and only if the \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\) are, and that the condition of separability is satisfied if and only if it is so for \(\mathscr{B}_0\), or again for all the \(\mathscr{B}_n\). Thus, \(\mathscr{B}\) is étale over \(S\) if and only if the \(\mathscr{B}_n\) over the \(S_n\) are. Taking this into account, 4 follows immediately from 3.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

It was not necessary in 4 to restrict to the case of coverings. It is however the only one used for the moment.

pages.title: Permanence properties
pages.html:
9 Permanence properties

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local and étale homomorphism; we examine here some cases in which a certain property for \(A\) entails the same property for \(B\), or conversely. A number of such propositions are already consequences of the mere fact that \(B\) is quasi-finite and flat over \(A\), and we shall confine ourselves to “recalling” a few of them: \(A\) and \(B\) have the same Krull dimension, and the same depth (Serre's “cohomological codimension”, in the still current terminology). It follows for example that \(A\) is Cohen-Macaulay if and only if \(B\) is. Moreover, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\), inducing \(\mathfrak{p}\) on \(A\), \(B_{\mathfrak{q}}\) will again be quasi-finite and flat over \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\), provided one assumes that \(B\) is a localization of an algebra of finite type over \(A\) (this follows from the fact that the set of points where a morphism of finite type is quasi-finite resp. flat is open); and moreover every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) is induced by a prime ideal \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) (for \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\)). It follows for example that \(\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\) have the same rank; and again that \(A\) has no embedded prime ideal if and only if \(B\) does.

We shall therefore confine ourselves to the propositions more special to the case of étale morphisms.

Proposition 1

Let \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local étale homomorphism. In order that \(A\) be regular, it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be so.

Indeed, let \(k\) be the residue field of \(A\), \(L\) that of \(B\). Since \(B\) is flat over \(A\) and \(L=B\otimes_A k\), i.e. \(\mathfrak{n}=\mathfrak{m}B\) (where \(\mathfrak{m}\), \(\mathfrak{n}\) are the maximal ideals of \(A\), \(B\)) the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic filtration on \(B\) is identical to its \(\mathfrak{n}\)-adic filtration and we shall have

\[ \operatorname{gr}^*(B)=\operatorname{gr}^*(A)\otimes_k L. \]

It follows that \(\operatorname{gr}^*(B)\) is a polynomial algebra over \(L\) if and only if \(\operatorname{gr}^*(A)\) is a polynomial algebra over \(k\). q.e.d. (N.B. we have not used the fact that \(L/k\) is separable).

Corollary 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an étale morphism. If \(Y\) is regular, so is \(X\), the converse being true if \(f\) is surjective.

Proposition 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an étale morphism. If \(Y\) is reduced, the same holds for \(X\), the converse being true if \(f\) is surjective.

This is equivalent to

Corollary 3

Let \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local étale homomorphism, \(B\) being isomorphic to a localized \(A\)-algebra of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type. In order that \(A\) be reduced, it is necessary and sufficient that \(B\) be so.

Necessity is trivial, since \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) is injective (\(B\) being faithfully flat over \(A\)). Sufficiency: let \(\mathfrak{p}_i\) be the minimal prime ideals of \(A\); by hypothesis the natural map \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\prod_i A/\mathfrak{p}_i\) is injective, hence tensoring with the flat \(A\)-module \(B\), we find that \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\prod_i B/\mathfrak{p}_iB\) is injective, and we are reduced to proving that the \(B/\mathfrak{p}_iB\) are reduced. Since \(B/\mathfrak{p}_iB\) is étale over \(A/\mathfrak{p}_i\), we are reduced to the case \(A\) integral. Let \(K\) be its field of fractions; then \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) being injective, the same holds (\(B\) being \(A\)-flat) for \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\otimes_A K\), and we are reduced to proving that this latter ring is reduced. Now, \(B\) being a localization of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type over \(A\), is the local ring of a point \(x\) of a scheme of finite type and étale \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(C)\) over \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), hence \(B\otimes_A K\) is a localized ring (with respect to a suitable multiplicatively stable set) of the ring \(C\otimes_A K\) of \(X\otimes_A K\). Since \(X\otimes_A K\) is étale over \(K\), its ring is a finite product of fields (separable extensions of \(K\)); hence the same holds for \(B\otimes_A K\), q.e.d.

Corollary 4

Let \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local étale homomorphism, suppose \(A\) analytically reduced (i.e. the completion \(\widehat{A}\) of \(A\) without nilpotent elements). Then \(B\) is analytically reduced, and a fortiori reduced.

Indeed, \(\widehat{B}\) is finite and étale over \(\widehat{A}\), and one applies 3.

Theorem 5

Let \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) be a local homomorphism, \(B\) being isomorphic to a localized algebra of an \(A\)-algebra of finite type. Then:

  1. (i)

    If \(f\) is étale, \(A\) is normal if and only if \(B\) is.

  2. (ii)

    If \(A\) is normal, \(f\) is étale if and only if \(f\) is injective and unramified (net) (and then \(B\) is normal by (i)).

We shall give two different proofs of (i), the first uses certain of the properties of flat quasi-finite morphisms (recalled at the beginning of the section) without using 6 (and thereby, the Main Theorem); it is the reverse for the second proof. Finally, for (ii) it seems that one needs the Main Theorem in any case.

First proof

One uses the following necessary and sufficient condition for normality of a Noetherian local ring \(A\) of dimension \(\neq0\).

Serre's Criterion

(i) For every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) of rank \(1\), \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is normal (or what amounts to the same, regular); (ii) For every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) of rank \(\geqslant 2\), we have depth \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\) \(\geqslant2\).1

We shall admit here this criterion, which is supposed to appear in the section on flat morphisms. Its principal advantage is that it does not assume a priori that \(A\) is reduced, nor a fortiori integral. Here, we may already suppose \(\dim{A}=\dim{B}\neq0\).

According to the recollections at the beginning of the section, the prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) which are of rank \(1\) (resp. of rank \(\geqslant2\)) are exactly the traces on \(A\) of the prime ideals \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(B\) which are of rank \(1\) (resp. of rank \(\geqslant2\)). Finally, if \(\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\mathfrak{q}\) correspond, \(B_{\mathfrak{q}}\) is étale over \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\), hence has the same depth as \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and is regular if and only if \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is (1). Applying Serre's criterion, we find that \(A\) is normal if and only if \(B\) is.

Second proof

Suppose \(B\) normal, let \(L\) be its field of fractions, \(K\) that of \(A\) (\(A\) is integral since \(B\) is). We saw in the proof of 3 that \(B\otimes_A K\) is a finite composite of fields; since it is contained in \(L\) it is a field, and since it contains \(B\) it is \(L\). An element of \(K\) integral over \(A\) is integral over \(B\), hence lies in \(B\) since \(B\) is normal, hence in \(A\) since \(B\cap K=A\) (as follows from the fact that \(B\) is faithfully flat over \(A\)).

Suppose now \(A\) normal, let us prove that \(B\) is so. By virtue of 6 we will have \(B=B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}}\), where \(B^\prime=A[t]/FA[t]\), \(F\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) being as in 6. Thus \(L=B\otimes_A K\) will be a localization of \(B^\prime\otimes_A K=K[t]/FK[t]\), and a product of fields, finite separable extensions of \(K\); this last product (as every time one localizes an Artinian ring, here \(B^\prime_K\) with respect to a multiplicatively stable set) is a direct factor of \(B^\prime_K\), thus corresponding to a decomposition \(F=F_1F_2\) in \(K[t]\), the generator of \(L\) corresponding to \(t\) being already annihilated by \(F_1\). Now, \(A\) being normal, the \(F_i\) are in \(A[t]\) (supposing that they are monic). Remarking that \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L=B\otimes_A K\) is injective (\(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K\) being so and \(B\) being flat over \(A\)) it follows that we will already have \(F_1(u)=0\), with \(u\) the class of \(t\) in \(L\). Supposing that we have taken \(F\) of minimal degree, it will follow that \(F_2=1\) (N.B. we will have \(F^\prime(u)=F^\prime_1(u)F_2(u)+F_1(u)F^\prime_2(u)=F^\prime_1(u)F_2(u)\) since \(F_1(u)=0\), whence \(F^\prime_1(u)\neq0\) since \(F^\prime(u)\neq0\).)

Thus we have

\[ \tag{*} L=B\otimes_A K=K[t]/FK[t] \]

\(F\) being consequently a separable polynomial in \(K[t]\) (but obviously not necessarily in \(A[t]\)). (N.B. For the moment, we have only shown, essentially, that in 6 we can choose \(F\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) in such a way that — with the notations taken here — \(B^\prime\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}}=B\) is injective; for this we have made use of the normality of \(A\); I do not know whether this remains true without a normality hypothesis).

Let us now recall the well-known lemma, taken from Serre's Course of last year:

Lemma 6

Let \(K\) be a ring, \(F\in K[t]\) a monic separable polynomial, \(L=K[t]/FK[t]\), \(u\) the class of \(t\) in \(L\) (so that \(F^\prime(u)\) is an invertible element of \(L\)). Then we have the formulas (where \(n=\deg{F}\)):

  1. \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K} u^i/F^\prime(u)=0\) if \(0\leqslant i< n-1\),

  2. \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K} u^{n-1}/F^\prime(u)=1\).

Corollary 7

The determinant of the matrix \((u^j\cdot u^i/F^\prime(u))_{0\leqslant i,j\leqslant n-1}\) is equal to \((-1)^{n(n-1)/2}\), hence invertible in every subring \(A\) of \(K\).

Corollary 8

Let \(A\) be a subring of \(K\), \(V\) the \(A\)-module generated by the \(u^i\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)) in \(L\), \(V^\prime\) the sub-\(A\)-module of \(L\) formed of the \(x\in L\) such that \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K}(xy)\in A\) for all \(y\in V\) (i.e. for \(y\) of the form \(u^i\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)). Then \(V^\prime\) is the \(A\)-module having as basis the \(u^i/F^\prime(u)\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)).

Corollary 9

Suppose that \(K\) is the field of fractions of a normal integral ring \(A\), \(F\) having its coefficients in \(A\). Then with the notations of 8, \(V^\prime\) contains the normal closure \(A^\prime\) of \(A\) in \(L\), which is therefore contained in \(A[u]/F^\prime(u)\) and a fortiori in \(A[u][F^\prime(u)^{-1}]\).

Let us apply this last corollary to the situation we had obtained in the proof: since \(F^\prime(u)\) is invertible in \(B\) which contains \(A[u]\), \(B\) contains \(A^\prime\). By the Main Theorem, (or from the fact that \(B=A[u]_{\mathfrak{n}}\)) \(B\) is a localized algebra of \(A^\prime\). Since \(A^\prime\) is normal, so is \(B\).

Proof of (ii)

We proceed as in the preceding proof to prove that we can, in 6, choose \(F\) in such a way that we still have (\(*\)). The only obstacle a priori is that, \(B\) being no longer supposed flat over \(A\), we can no longer assert that \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\) is injective, so that the reasoning will a priori apply only to the image \(B_1\) of \(B\) under the said homomorphism. It follows immediately that \(B_1\) is flat over \(A\) (as a localization of a free algebra over \(A\)). By virtue of 8 the morphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) is étale, hence an isomorphism, which completes the proof.

(From the point of view of exposition, one should interchange the two last proofs, and put in a separate number the formal calculations of the lemma and its corollaries).

Corollary 10

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an étale morphism. If \(Y\) is normal, \(X\) is so, the converse being true if \(f\) is surjective.

Corollary 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a dominant morphism, \(Y\) being normal and \(X\) connected. If \(f\) is unramified (net), \(f\) is étale, hence \(X\) is normal and consequently (being connected) irreducible.

Let \(U\) be the set of points where \(f\) is étale; it is open, and it suffices to show that it is also closed and non-empty. \(U\) contains the inverse image of the generic point of \(Y\) (for, for an algebra over a field, unramified \(=\) étale) hence (\(X\) dominating \(Y\)) is non-empty. If \(x\) belongs to the closure of \(U\), then it belongs to the closure of an irreducible component \(U_i\) of \(U\), hence to an irreducible component \(X_i\overset{v}{=}\overline{U}_i\) of \(X\) which meets \(U\), and consequently dominates \(Y\) (for every component of \(U\), being flat over \(Y\), dominates \(Y\)). Consequently, if \(y\) is the projection of \(x\) onto \(Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_x\) is injective (taking into account that \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is integral). Since \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is normal and \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_x\) unramified (net), we conclude by means of 5(ii).

Corollary 12

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a dominant morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) normal and \(X\) irreducible. Then the set of points where \(f\) is étale is identical to the complement of the support of \(\Omega^1_{X/Y}\), i.e. to the complement of the sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by the different ideal \(\mathfrak{d}_{X/Y}\).

(This is the “less trivial” statement that was alluded to in the remark of 4.)

Remark

One should beware of believing that a connected étale covering of an irreducible scheme is itself irreducible, when one does not suppose the base normal. This question will be studied in 11.

One uses the following necessary and sufficient condition for normality of a Noetherian local ring \(A\) of dimension \(\neq0\).
