Supposing that we have taken \(F\) of minimal degree, it will follow that \(F_2=1\) (N.B. we will have \(F^\prime(u)=F^\prime_1(u)F_2(u)+F_1(u)F^\prime_2(u)=F^\prime_1(u)F_2(u)\) since \(F_1(u)=0\), whence \(F^\prime_1(u)\neq0\) since \(F^\prime(u)\neq0\).)
Thus we have
\[ \tag{*} L=B\otimes_A K=K[t]/FK[t] \]

\(F\) being consequently a separable polynomial in \(K[t]\) (but obviously not necessarily in \(A[t]\)). (N.B. For the moment, we have only shown, essentially, that in 6 we can choose \(F\) and \(\mathfrak{n}\) in such a way that — with the notations taken here — \(B^\prime\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}}=B\) is injective; for this we have made use of the normality of \(A\); I do not know whether this remains true without a normality hypothesis).
Let us now recall the well-known lemma, taken from Serre's Course of last year:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6

Let \(K\) be a ring, \(F\in K[t]\) a monic separable polynomial, \(L=K[t]/FK[t]\), \(u\) the class of \(t\) in \(L\) (so that \(F^\prime(u)\) is an invertible element of \(L\)). Then we have the formulas (where \(n=\deg{F}\)):

  1. \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K} u^i/F^\prime(u)=0\) if \(0\leqslant i< n-1\),

  2. \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K} u^{n-1}/F^\prime(u)=1\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

The determinant of the matrix \((u^j\cdot u^i/F^\prime(u))_{0\leqslant i,j\leqslant n-1}\) is equal to \((-1)^{n(n-1)/2}\), hence invertible in every subring \(A\) of \(K\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(A\) be a subring of \(K\), \(V\) the \(A\)-module generated by the \(u^i\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)) in \(L\), \(V^\prime\) the sub-\(A\)-module of \(L\) formed of the \(x\in L\) such that \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K}(xy)\in A\) for all \(y\in V\) (i.e. for \(y\) of the form \(u^i\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)). Then \(V^\prime\) is the \(A\)-module having as basis the \(u^i/F^\prime(u)\) (\(0\leqslant i\leqslant n-1\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Suppose that \(K\) is the field of fractions of a normal integral ring \(A\), \(F\) having its coefficients in \(A\). Then with the notations of 8, \(V^\prime\) contains the normal closure \(A^\prime\) of \(A\) in \(L\), which is therefore contained in \(A[u]/F^\prime(u)\) and a fortiori in \(A[u][F^\prime(u)^{-1}]\).

Let us apply this last corollary to the situation we had obtained in the proof: since \(F^\prime(u)\) is invertible in \(B\) which contains \(A[u]\), \(B\) contains \(A^\prime\). By the Main Theorem, (or from the fact that \(B=A[u]_{\mathfrak{n}}\)) \(B\) is a localized algebra of \(A^\prime\). Since \(A^\prime\) is normal, so is \(B\).
Proof of (ii)

We proceed as in the preceding proof to prove that we can, in 6, choose \(F\) in such a way that we still have (\(*\)). The only obstacle a priori is that, \(B\) being no longer supposed flat over \(A\), we can no longer assert that \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L\) is injective, so that the reasoning will a priori apply only to the image \(B_1\) of \(B\) under the said homomorphism. It follows immediately that \(B_1\) is flat over \(A\) (as a localization of a free algebra over \(A\)). By virtue of 8 the morphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B_1\) is étale, hence an isomorphism, which completes the proof.
(From the point of view of exposition, one should interchange the two last proofs, and put in a separate number the formal calculations of the lemma and its corollaries).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an étale morphism. If \(Y\) is normal, \(X\) is so, the converse being true if \(f\) is surjective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a dominant morphism, \(Y\) being normal and \(X\) connected. If \(f\) is unramified (net), \(f\) is étale, hence \(X\) is normal and consequently (being connected) irreducible.

Let \(U\) be the set of points where \(f\) is étale; it is open, and it suffices to show that it is also closed and non-empty. \(U\) contains the inverse image of the generic point of \(Y\) (for, for an algebra over a field, unramified \(=\) étale) hence (\(X\) dominating \(Y\)) is non-empty. If \(x\) belongs to the closure of \(U\), then it belongs to the closure of an irreducible component \(U_i\) of \(U\), hence to an irreducible component \(X_i\overset{v}{=}\overline{U}_i\) of \(X\) which meets \(U\), and consequently dominates \(Y\) (for every component of \(U\), being flat over \(Y\), dominates \(Y\)). Consequently, if \(y\) is the projection of \(x\) onto \(Y\), \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_x\) is injective (taking into account that \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is integral). Since \(\mathscr{O}_y\) is normal and \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_x\) unramified (net), we conclude by means of 5(ii).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a dominant morphism of finite type, with \(Y\) normal and \(X\) irreducible. Then the set of points where \(f\) is étale is identical to the complement of the support of \(\Omega^1_{X/Y}\), i.e. to the complement of the sub-prescheme of \(X\) defined by the different ideal \(\mathfrak{d}_{X/Y}\).
(This is the “less trivial” statement that was alluded to in the remark of 4.)

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

One should beware of believing that a connected étale covering of an irreducible scheme is itself irreducible, when one does not suppose the base normal. This question will be studied in 11.

pages.title: Étale coverings of a normal scheme
pages.html:
10 Étale coverings of a normal scheme

Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme étale and separated over \(Y\) normal connected with field \(K\). Then the connected components

\(X_i\) of \(X\) are integral, their fields \(K_i\) are finite separable extensions of \(K\), \(X_i\) is identified with a non-empty open subset of the normalization of \(X\) in \(K_i\) (hence \(X\) with a dense open subset of the normalization of \(Y\) in \(R(X)=L=\prod K_i\)).

By 10 \(X\) is normal, a fortiori its local rings are integral, hence the connected components of \(X\) are irreducible. Since \(X_i\) is normal, and finite and dominant over \(Y\), it follows from a particular case (almost trivial moreover) of the Main Theorem that \(X_i\) is an open subset of the normalization of \(X\) in the field \(K_i\) of \(X\).

Corollary 2

Under the conditions 1, \(X\) is finite over \(Y\) (i.e. an étale covering of \(Y\)) if and only if \(X\) is isomorphic to the normalization \(Y'\) of \(Y\) in \(L=R(X)\) (ring of rational functions on \(X\)).

Indeed, one knows that this normalization is finite over \(Y\) (\(Y\) being normal and \(R/K\) separable), conversely if \(X\) is finite over \(Y\) it is so over \(Y'\), hence its image in \(Y'\) is closed, on the other hand it is dense.

An algebra \(L\) of finite rank over \(K\) will be said to be unramified over \(X\) (or simply unramified over \(K\), if \(X\) is understood) if \(L\) is a separable algebra over \(K\), i.e. a direct composite of separable extensions \(K_i\), and if the normalization \(Y'\) of \(Y\) in \(L\) (disjoint sum of the normalizations of \(Y\) in the \(K_i\)) is unramified (\(=\) étale by 11) over \(Y\). Hence:

Corollary 3

For every \(X\) finite over \(Y\) and each of whose irreducible components dominates \(Y\), let \(R(X)\) be the ring of rational functions on \(X\) (product of the local rings of the generic points of the irreducible components of \(X\)), so that \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} R(X)\) is a functor, with values in the algebras of finite rank over \(K=R(Y)\). This functor establishes an equivalence of the category of connected étale coverings of \(Y\) with the category of extensions \(L\) of \(K\) unramified over \(Y\).

The inverse functor is the normalization functor.

Suppose \(Y\) affine, hence defined by a normal ring \(A\) with field of fractions \(K\). Let \(L\) be a finite extension of \(K\), a direct composite of fields, then by definition the normalization \(Y'\) of \(Y\) in \(L\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), where \(A'\) is the normalization of \(A\) in \(L\). To say that \(L\) is unramified over \(Y\) means that \(A'\) is unramified (or again: étale) over \(A\). If \(A\) is local, it amounts to the same to say that the local rings \(A^\prime_{\mathfrak{n}}\) (where \(\mathfrak{n}\) runs through the finite set of maximal ideals of \(A'\), i.e. of its prime ideals inducing the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of \(A\)) are unramified (\(=\) étale) over

the local ring \(A\). Finally, let us also note that the criterion by the discriminant (10) can also be applied in this situation (more generally, a variant of the said criterion ought to be stated thus, without a preliminary condition of flatness when \(X\) dominates \(Y\), \(Y\) being nevertheless assumed locally integral: \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) and \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\otimes_A K =L\) are injective — then \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K}\) is defined — and \(\operatorname{tr}_{L/K}(xy)\) induces a fundamental bilinear form \(B\times B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\), i.e. there exist \(x_i\in B\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant n\), \(n=\) rank of \(L\) over \(K\)) such that \(\operatorname{tr}(x_ix_j)\in A\) for all \(i\), \(j\) and \(\det(\operatorname{tr}(x_ix_j))\) is invertible in \(A\)).

The sorites (4.6) immediately implies the sorites of unramification in the classical framework:

Proposition 4

Let \(Y\) be a normal integral prescheme, with field \(K\). (i) \(K\) is unramified over \(Y\). (ii) If \(L\) is an extension of \(K\) unramified over \(Y\), if \(Y'\) is a normal prescheme with field \(L\) and dominating \(Y\) (for example the normalization of \(Y\) in \(L\)) and \(M\) an extension of \(L\) unramified over \(Y'\), then \(M/K\) is unramified over \(X\) (transitivity of unramification). (iii) Let \(Y'\) be a normal integral prescheme dominating \(Y\), with field \(K'/K\); if \(L\) is an extension of \(K\) unramified over \(Y\), then \(L\otimes_K K'\) is an extension of \(K'\) unramified over \(Y'\) (property of translation).

Moreover:

Corollary 5

Under the conditions of (iii), if \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), then the normalization \(\overline{A'}\) of \(Y'\) in \(L'=L\otimes_K K'\) is identified with \(\overline{A}\otimes_A A'\), where \(\overline{A}\) is the normalization of \(A\) in \(L\).

Usually, people (who are reluctant to consider non-integral rings, even were they direct composites of fields) state the translation property in the following (weaker) form:

Corollary 6

Under the conditions of (iii), let \(L_1\) be a composite extension of \(L/K\) (unramified over \(Y\)) and of \(K'/K\). Then \(L_1/K'\) is unramified over \(Y'\). In the case where \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(A')\), one will have moreover

\[ \overline{A'}=A[\overline{A},A'] \]

i.e. the ring \(\overline{A'}\) normalization of \(A'\) in \(L_1\) is the \(A\)-algebra generated by \(A'\) and the normalization \(\overline{A}\) of \(A\) in \(L\).

This last fact is moreover false without the hypothesis of unramification, even in the case of composite extensions of number fields...

To end this article, we are going to give the interpretation of the notion of étale covering corresponding to the intuitive image of this notion: there must be the “maximum number” of points above the point \(y\in Y\) under consideration, and in particular there must not be “several coincident points” above \(y\). To prove the results in this direction with all the desirable generality, we are going to admit here the proposition 7 below (whose proof will be in the multiplodoque, Chap. IV, par. 15, and uses the technique of Chevalley's constructible sets, and a little bit of descent theory...).

A morphism of finite type \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is said to be universally open if for every base extension \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) (with \(Y'\) locally Noetherian) the morphism \(f'\colon X'=X\times_Y Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) is open, i.e. transforms open sets into open sets. One may moreover restrict to the case where \(Y'\) is of finite type over \(Y\) (and even where \(Y'\) is of the form \(Y[t_1,\dots,t_r]\), where the \(t_i\) are indeterminates). A universally open morphism is a fortiori open (the converse being false), on the other hand if \(f\) is open, \(X\) and \(Y\) being irreducible, then all the components of all the fibres of \(f\) have the same dimension (namely the dimension of the generic fibre \(f^{-1}(z)\), \(z\) the generic point of \(Y\)). Finally if \(Y\) is normal, this last condition already implies that \(f\) is universally open (Chevalley's theorem). It follows for example that if \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a quasi-finite morphism, with \(Y\) normal irreducible, then \(f\) is universally open (or again: open) if and only if every irreducible component of \(X\) dominates \(Y\). Let us also recall that a flat morphism (of finite type) being open, is also universally open. These preliminaries being settled, let us “recall” the

Proposition 7

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite separated universally open morphism. For every \(y\in Y\), let \(n(y)\) be the “geometric number of points of the fibre \(f^{-1}(y)\)”, equal to the sum of the separable degrees of the residual extensions \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(y)\), for the points \(x\in f^{-1}(y)\). Then the function \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) on \(Y\) is upper semi-continuous. For it to be constant in the neighbourhood of the point \(y\) (i.e. for one to have \(n(y)=n(z_i)\), where the \(z_i\) are the generic points of the irreducible components of \(Y\) which contain \(y\)) it is necessary that there exist a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) be finite over \(U\).1

Corollary 8

If \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) is constant and \(Y\) is geometrically unibranch2, the irreducible components of \(X\) are disjoint.

Proposition 9

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a separated étale morphism. With the notation of 7, the function \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) is upper semi-continuous. In order for it to be constant in the neighbourhood of the point \(y\) (i.e. in order to have \(n(y)=n(z_i)\), where the \(z_i\) are the generic points of the irreducible components of \(Y\) which contain \(y\)), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) be finite over U, i.e. be an étale covering of \(U\).

Corollary 10

In order for a separated étale morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), with \(Y\) connected, to be finite (i.e. to make \(X\) an étale covering of \(Y\)), it is necessary and sufficient that all the fibres of \(f\) have the same geometric number of points.

In 7 and its corollary, there was no normality hypothesis on \(Y\). If one makes such a hypothesis, one obtains the stronger statement (most often taken as the definition of the non-ramification of a covering):

Theorem 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a separated quasi-finite morphism. Suppose that \(Y\) is irreducible, that every component of \(X\) dominates \(Y\), and that \(X\) is reduced (i.e. \(\mathscr{O}_X\) without nilpotent elements). Let \(n\) be the degree of \(X\) over \(Y\) (the sum of the degrees, over the field \(K\) of \(Y\), of the fields \(K_i\) of the irreducible components \(X_i\) of \(X\)). Let \(y\) be a normal point of \(Y\). Then the geometric number \(n(y)\) of points of \(X\) above \(y\) is \(\leqslant n\), with equality holding if and only if there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) is an étale covering of \(U\).

The “only if” being trivial, let us prove the “if”. Let \(z\) be the generic point of \(Y\); we have \(n(z)=\) (the sum of the separable degrees of the \(K_i/K\)) \(\leqslant n\), and by 7 we have \(n(y)\leqslant n(z)\leqslant n\), the equality implying that \(X|U\) is finite over \(U\) for a suitable neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\). We may therefore suppose \(X\) finite over \(Y\) and the function \(n(y')\) on \(Y\) constant. Finally, by 8, \(X\) is then the disjoint union of its irreducible components, and to prove that it is unramified at \(y\), we are reduced to the case where \(X\) is irreducible, hence integral. Finally we may suppose \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_y)\). The theorem then reduces to the following classical statement:

Corollary 12

Let \(A\) be a normal local ring (Noetherian as always) with field \(K\), let \(L\) be a finite extension of \(K\) of degree \(n\) and separable degree \(n_s\), let \(B\) be a subring of \(L\) finite over \(A\), with field of fractions \(L\), let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of \(A\) and \(n'\) the separable degree of \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) over \(A/\mathfrak{m}A=k\) (\(=\) the sum of the separable degrees of the residual extensions of this ring). We have \(n'\leqslant n_s\) and a fortiori \(n'\leqslant n\). This last inequality is an equality if and only if \(B\) is unramified (\(=\) étale) over \(A\).

It remains only to show that \(n'=n\) implies that \(B\) is étale over \(A\). Let us recall the proof when \(k\) is infinite: one need only show that \(R=B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is separable over \(k\); if this were not so, it would follow (by a known lemma) that there exists an element \(a\) of \(R\) whose minimal polynomial over \(k\) is of degree \(>n'\). This element comes from an element \(x\) of \(B\), whose minimal polynomial over \(K\) (as an element of \(L\)) is of degree \(\leqslant n\); on the other hand, this latter has its coefficients in \(A\) since \(A\) is normal, and hence gives, by reduction mod \(\mathfrak{m}\), a monic polynomial \(F\in k[t]\), of degree \(\leqslant n=n'\), such that \(F(a)=0\), which is absurd.

In the general case (\(k\) possibly being finite), reverting to geometric language, one considers \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(A[t])\), which is faithfully flat over \(Y\), and the generic point \(y'\) of the fibre \(\operatorname{Spec}(k[t])\) of \(Y'\) over \(y\). Then \(X\) is unramified over \(Y\) at \(y\) if and only if \(X'=X\times_Y Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(B[t])\) is unramified at \(y'\) over \(Y'\), as one verifies immediately. On the other hand, by the choice of \(y'\), its residue field is \(k(t)\), hence infinite. Since \(y'\) is a normal point of \(Y'\), we are reduced to the preceding case.

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite separated universally open morphism. For every \(y\in Y\), let \(n(y)\) be the “geometric number of points of the fibre \(f^{-1}(y)\)”, equal to the sum of the separable degrees of the residual extensions \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(y)\), for the points \(x\in f^{-1}(y)\). Then the function \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) on \(Y\) is upper semi-continuous. For it to be constant in the neighbourhood of the point \(y\) (i.e. for one to have \(n(y)=n(z_i)\), where the \(z_i\) are the generic points of the irreducible components of \(Y\) which contain \(y\)) it is necessary that there exist a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) be finite over \(U\).1

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

If \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) is constant and \(Y\) is geometrically unibranch2, the irreducible components of \(X\) are disjoint.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a separated étale morphism. With the notation of 7, the function \(y\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} n(y)\) is upper semi-continuous. In order for it to be constant in the neighbourhood of the point \(y\) (i.e. in order to have \(n(y)=n(z_i)\), where the \(z_i\) are the generic points of the irreducible components of \(Y\) which contain \(y\)), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) be finite over U, i.e. be an étale covering of \(U\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

In order for a separated étale morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), with \(Y\) connected, to be finite (i.e. to make \(X\) an étale covering of \(Y\)), it is necessary and sufficient that all the fibres of \(f\) have the same geometric number of points.

In 7 and its corollary, there was no normality hypothesis on \(Y\). If one makes such a hypothesis, one obtains the stronger statement (most often taken as the definition of the non-ramification of a covering):

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 11

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a separated quasi-finite morphism. Suppose that \(Y\) is irreducible, that every component of \(X\) dominates \(Y\), and that \(X\) is reduced (i.e. \(\mathscr{O}_X\) without nilpotent elements). Let \(n\) be the degree of \(X\) over \(Y\) (the sum of the degrees, over the field \(K\) of \(Y\), of the fields \(K_i\) of the irreducible components \(X_i\) of \(X\)). Let \(y\) be a normal point of \(Y\). Then the geometric number \(n(y)\) of points of \(X\) above \(y\) is \(\leqslant n\), with equality holding if and only if there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\) such that \(X|U\) is an étale covering of \(U\).

The “only if” being trivial, let us prove the “if”. Let \(z\) be the generic point of \(Y\); we have \(n(z)=\) (the sum of the separable degrees of the \(K_i/K\)) \(\leqslant n\), and by 7 we have \(n(y)\leqslant n(z)\leqslant n\), the equality implying that \(X|U\) is finite over \(U\) for a suitable neighbourhood \(U\) of \(y\). We may therefore suppose \(X\) finite over \(Y\) and the function \(n(y')\) on \(Y\) constant. Finally, by 8, \(X\) is then the disjoint union of its irreducible components, and to prove that it is unramified at \(y\), we are reduced to the case where \(X\) is irreducible, hence integral. Finally we may suppose \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_y)\). The theorem then reduces to the following classical statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12

Let \(A\) be a normal local ring (Noetherian as always) with field \(K\), let \(L\) be a finite extension of \(K\) of degree \(n\) and separable degree \(n_s\), let \(B\) be a subring of \(L\) finite over \(A\), with field of fractions \(L\), let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of \(A\) and \(n'\) the separable degree of \(B/\mathfrak{m}B\) over \(A/\mathfrak{m}A=k\) (\(=\) the sum of the separable degrees of the residual extensions of this ring). We have \(n'\leqslant n_s\) and a fortiori \(n'\leqslant n\). This last inequality is an equality if and only if \(B\) is unramified (\(=\) étale) over \(A\).

It remains only to show that \(n'=n\) implies that \(B\) is étale over \(A\). Let us recall the proof when \(k\) is infinite: one need only show that \(R=B/\mathfrak{m}B\) is separable over \(k\); if this were not so, it would follow (by a known lemma) that there exists an element \(a\) of \(R\) whose minimal polynomial over \(k\) is of degree \(>n'\). This element comes from an element \(x\) of \(B\), whose minimal polynomial over \(K\) (as an element of \(L\)) is of degree \(\leqslant n\); on the other hand, this latter has its coefficients in \(A\) since \(A\) is normal, and hence gives, by reduction mod \(\mathfrak{m}\), a monic polynomial \(F\in k[t]\), of degree \(\leqslant n=n'\), such that \(F(a)=0\), which is absurd.

In the general case (\(k\) possibly being finite), reverting to geometric language, one considers \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(A[t])\), which is faithfully flat over \(Y\), and the generic point \(y'\) of the fibre \(\operatorname{Spec}(k[t])\) of \(Y'\) over \(y\). Then \(X\) is unramified over \(Y\) at \(y\) if and only if \(X'=X\times_Y Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(B[t])\) is unramified at \(y'\) over \(Y'\), as one verifies immediately. On the other hand, by the choice of \(y'\), its residue field is \(k(t)\), hence infinite. Since \(y'\) is a normal point of \(Y'\), we are reduced to the preceding case.

footnotes.html: For the definition, cf. below 11, p.

pages.title: Some complements
pages.html:
11 Some complements

We have already said that a connected étale covering of an integral scheme is not necessarily integral. Here are two examples of this fact.

a) Let \(C\) be an algebraic curve with an ordinary double point \(x\), \(C^\prime\) its normalization, \(a\) and \(b\) the two points of \(C^\prime\) above \(x\). Let \(C_i^\prime\) (\(i=1\), \(2\)) be two copies of \(C^\prime\), \(a_i\) and \(b_i\) the point of \(C_i^\prime\) corresponding to \(a\) resp. \(b\). In the sum curve \(C_1^\prime \amalg C_2^\prime\), let us identify \(a_1\) and \(b_2\) on the one hand, \(a_2\) and \(b_1\) on the other hand (we leave to the reader the task of making precise the identification process; it will be explained in Chap. VI of the multiplodocus but, in the case of curves over an algebraically closed field, it is treated in Serre's book on algebraic curves). One obtains a curve \(C^{\prime\prime}\) which is connected and reducible, and which is an étale covering of degree \(2\) of \(C\). The reader will verify that in general, the connected “Galois” étale coverings \(C^{\prime\prime}\) of \(C\) whose inverse image \(C^{\prime\prime} \times_C C^\prime\) is a trivial covering of \(C^\prime\) (i.e. isomorphic to the sum of a certain number of copies of \(C^\prime\)) are “cyclic” of degree \(n\), and for every integer \(n>0\), one can construct a connected cyclic étale covering of degree \(n\). In the language of the fundamental group which will be developed later, this means that the quotient of \(\pi_1(C)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the image of \(\pi_1(C^\prime) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(C)\) (the homomorphism induced by the projection) is isomorphic to the compactification of \(\mathbf{Z}\). More precisely, one should be able to show that the fundamental group of \(C\) is isomorphic to the free (topological) product of the fundamental group of \(C^\prime\) by the compactification of \(\mathbf{Z}\). Let us note that it is questions of this kind which gave rise to the “theory of descent” for schemes.

b) Let \(A\) be a complete integral local ring; one knows that its normalization \(A^\prime\) is finite over \(A\) (Nagata), hence it is a complete semi-local ring, hence local since it is integral. Suppose that the residual extension \(L/k\) which it defines is non-radicial (in the contrary case, one will say that \(A\) is geometrically unibranch, cf. below). This will be the case for example for the ring \(\mathbf{R}[[s,t]]/(s^2+t^2)\mathbf{R}[[s,t]]\), where \(\mathbf{R}\) is the field of reals. Let then \(k^\prime\) be a finite Galois extension of \(k\) such that \(L \otimes_k k^\prime\) decomposes; and let \(B\) be a finite étale algebra over \(A\) corresponding to the residual extension \(k^\prime\) (recall that \(B\) is essentially unique). Then \(B^\prime = A^\prime \otimes_A B\) over \(B\) has residual algebra \(L \otimes_k k^\prime\) which is not local, hence \(B^\prime\) is not a local ring, hence (being complete) has divisors of \(0\). Since it is contained in the total ring of fractions of \(B\) (for being free over \(A^\prime\) hence torsion-free over \(A^\prime\) hence torsion-free over \(A\), hence contained in \(B^\prime \otimes_A K = B^\prime_{(K)} = A^\prime_{(K)} \otimes_K B_{(K)} = B_{(K)}\) since \(A^\prime_{(K)} = K\)) it follows that \(B\) is not integral. In the case of the ring \(\mathbf{R}[s,t]/(s^2+t^2)\mathbf{R}[s,t]\), taking \(k^\prime/k= \mathbf{C}/\mathbf{R}\), one finds for \(B\) the local ring of two secant lines of the plane at their point of intersection.

Let us note moreover that if there exists a connected étale covering \(X\) of an integral \(Y\) which is not irreducible, then every irreducible component of \(X\) gives an example of an unramified covering \(X^\prime\) of \(Y\), dominating \(Y\), which is not étale over \(Y\). In the case of example a), one obtains in this way that \(C^\prime\) is unramified over \(C\), without being étale at the two points \(a\) and \(b\) (as one moreover verifies directly by inspection of the completions of the local rings of \(x\) and \(a\): from the “formal” point of view, \(C^\prime\) at the point \(a\) is identified with a closed subscheme of \(C\) at the point \(x\), namely one of the two “branches” of \(C\) passing through \(x\)).

In a) and b), one sees that the non-validity of the conclusions of 5. (i) and (ii) is directly linked to the fact that a point of \(Y\) “bursts” into distinct points of the normalization (in b), the fact that the residual extension is non-radicial must be interpreted geometrically in this way). More precisely, we shall say that an integral local ring \(A\) is geometrically unibranch if its normalization has only a single maximal ideal, the corresponding residual extension being radicial; a point \(y\) of an integral prescheme is said to be geometrically unibranch if its local ring is so. Examples: a normal point, an ordinary cusp of a curve, etc. It seems that if \(Y\) admits a point which is not unibranch, there always exists a connected étale covering of \(Y\) which is not irreducible; this is at least what we have shown in case b), when \(Y\) is the spectrum of a complete local ring. One can show on the other hand that if all the points of \(Y\) are geometrically unibranch, then every unramified connected \(Y\)-prescheme dominating \(Y\) is étale and irreducible. The proof takes up again that of 5, using the following generalization of the theorem 3, which will be proved later by means of the technique of descent1:

Let \(Y^\prime \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a finite, radicial, surjective morphism (i.e. what one might call a “universal homeomorphism”). Consider the functor \(X \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X \times_Y Y^\prime = X^\prime\) from \(Y\)-preschemes to \(Y^\prime\)-preschemes. This functor induces an equivalence of the category of étale \(Y\)-schemes with the category of étale \(Y^\prime\)-schemes. One may apply for example this result in the case where \(Y^\prime\) is the normalization of \(Y\), \(Y\) being assumed unibranch (and \(Y^\prime\) finite over \(Y\), which is true in all the cases one encounters in practice), or to the case of a \(Y^{\prime\prime}\) “in a sandwich” between \(Y\) and its normalization (which no longer needs to be finite over \(Y\)).
